ordinary_no_progress_guard.fingerprint: ordinary|**findings** 1. `must_fix` | scope / key decisions claim: “full `vfs`/`vfsfile` contract” and “rename redesign … close-open-handles → native `move()` … else copy → reopen-and-remap … preserving the trait’s ‘open handle stays bound’” ([prd](/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/prd/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-adapter.md:56), [prd](/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/prd/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-adapter.md:58)). why risky: the trait requires `rename` to succeed while a handle is open and keep that handle bound to the same data ([traits.rs](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/vfs/traits.rs:31)). opfs sync handles take exclusive file locks, and `move()` with an open sync handle is unresolved/unavailable as a contract; the whatwg pr explicitly discusses that an open sync handle blocks move. a close/reopen/remap is not equivalent for arbitrary `vfsfile` users. fix: either implement a real logical inode/path overlay that makes open handles independent of physical opfs paths, or state that opfs cannot satisfy posix rename-while-open and make a trait/test carveout explicit. do not claim “full contract” until the conformance test `rename_while_open_keeps_handle_alive` passes under real opfs. 2. `must_fix` | flow 3 / cross-tab safety claim: “`lock_exclusive(".writer.lock")` returns `pagedberror::alreadylocked` (fail-fast)” using web locks ([prd](/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/prd/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-adapter.md:35), [prd](/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/prd/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-adapter.md:60)). why risky: web locks are callback/promise-scoped. they queue by default; fail-fast only exists if `ifavailable` is used, where the callback receives `null` asynchronously. the rust raii handle must keep a js lock callback promise pending until drop; otherwise the lock releases immediately. spec lines show `ifavailable` null-callback behavior and queued processing. fix: specify the exact design: `navigator.locks.request(name, { mode, ifavailable: true }, async lock => { if (!lock) return null; await releasepromise; })`, map `null` to `alreadylocked`, and make `drop` resolve/reject `releasepromise`. add tests for contention, drop release, cancellation, tab/worker termination, and no default queued acquisition. 3. `must_fix` | core user flows / browser floor claim: “portable baseline: chrome/edge/firefox/safari on plain sync handles; chromium extras (`readwrite-unsafe`, `move()`) detected fast paths only” ([prd](/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/prd/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-adapter.md:95)). why risky: this conflicts with the multi-tab reader story. without shared/read-only sync handles, an observer or read-only tab cannot open `/main.db` while the writer worker holds a write sync handle. `pagedb` has `readonly` and `observer` modes with sentinel locks ([open.rs](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/txn/db/open.rs:468), [mode.rs](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/txn/mode.rs:11)); the prd does not decide whether opfs supports them through read-only handles, routes all readers through the owner worker, or disables them. fix: make v1 explicit: writer-only owner worker, or owner-worker-mediated readers, or require `createsyncaccesshandle({ mode: "read-only" })` and pin browser support. mdn currently documents `read-only`, `readwrite`, and `readwrite-unsafe`, while the file system standard excerpt still only describes bare exclusive `createsyncaccesshandle()`, so treat mode support as a compat gate, not a casual fast path. 4. `must_fix` | crash and reopen claim: “apply-journal replays pending segment promote/tombstone side effects” ([prd](/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/prd/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-adapter.md:42)). why wrong: normal `writetxn::commit` writes header fields with `apply_journal_root_page_id: 0` and then applies segment renames after the header ([write.rs](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/txn/write.rs:744), [write.rs](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/txn/write.rs:766)). the open path says no producer sets a non-zero journal page for existing code paths ([open.rs](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/txn/db/open.rs:338)). recovery for missing live segment relies on catalog reconciliation promoting staging ([reconcile.rs](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/recovery/reconcile.rs:70)). fix: rewrite the claim to say normal segment crash recovery depends on reconcile, not apply-journal. add oracle cuts specifically after header sync, before/after each segment rename, and during reconcile’s own `rename`. 5. `must_fix` | vfs contract / success criteria claim: “all-or-nothing vectored writes” ([prd](/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/prd/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-adapter.md:56)). why risky: opfs sync access handle writes are in-place. a vectored write implemented as sequential page writes can expose partial state if quota or an i/o exception fires midway. `pagedb` batches dirty pages then calls `write_at_vectored` before `sync()` ([core.rs](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/pager/core.rs:673)). header ordering can protect committed visibility, but it does not satisfy the trait’s “no partial state observable” contract. fix: either implement a real all-or-nothing strategy for vectored writes, such as staging plus swap where applicable, or narrow the vfs contract and success tests to the commit protocol’s actual tolerance of partial pre-header page writes. 6. `should_fix` | durability mapping claim: “`sync()` → `flush()` … commit-protocol ordering … preserved” and success criterion “full reliability” ([prd](/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/prd/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-adapter.md:59), [prd](/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/prd/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-adapter.md:135)). why risky: the file system standard says `flush()` attempts to transfer cached modifications and can be a no-op on some file systems; `close()` explicitly is not a durability guarantee. this supports “best effort,” not “full reliability.” fix: rename the criterion from “full reliability” to “browser-observed crash consistency under tested kill/reopen scenarios.” separate “logical atomicity after worker termination” from “power-loss durability.” 7. `should_fix` | typed failure surface claim: “quotaexceedederror as a typed i/o error — never a wasm trap; the engine aborts the write and the transaction rolls back cleanly” ([prd](/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/prd/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-adapter.md:48)). why risky: current `pagedberror` mapping in opfs only has coarse `io`, `readonly`, and a few filesystem kinds ([handle.rs](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/vfs/opfs/handle.rs:167)); the prd introduces `quotaexceeded` and `workerownership` without saying whether upstream error enums change. also, a quota error mid-vectored write may leave dirty pages already written. fix: define the exact upstream error enum additions or mapping strategy, then require tests for quota during create, page write, segment seal, header write, and rename/copy fallback. 8. `should_fix` | send/sync / wasm execution model claim: “on wasm32 the existing pattern is `unsafe impl send/sync` guarded by `send_wrapper` … a new backend inherits this obligation” ([prd](/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/prd/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-adapter.md:81)). why risky: the proposed mode 1 moves `db` inside a worker and mode 2 allows “rust async on a different thread.” the existing safety comments assume wasm32 has exactly one js thread ([vfs_impl.rs](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/vfs/opfs/vfs_impl.rs:140)), but sab/atomics and multi-worker bridge language undermine that assumption. fix: constrain `opfsvfs` to a single worker realm and make all cross-thread access go through a message protocol, not shared rust values. if shared wasm memory is in scope, the unsafe `send/sync` justification must be redesigned. 9. `suggestion` | scope / yagni claim: v1 includes mode 2 bridge, sab fast path, full browser oracle, benches, upstream pr, bootstrap, telemetry, doc drift fixes, and cross-browser suites ([prd](/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/prd/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-adapter.md:61)). why risky: the critical blocker is mode 1 correctness. mode 2, sab, npm-shaped bootstrap, and broad benchmark work multiply browser lifecycle and api surface before the storage contract is proven. fix: split v1 into “in-worker opfs vfs + conformance + crash oracle + upstream pr.” move mode 2/sab and packaging-shaped bootstrap to follow-up unless telpher/gitspen cannot integrate without them. **sources checked:** file system standard for sync-handle locking and `flush()`/`close()` semantics, web locks spec for `ifavailable` and queueing, mdn for dedicated-worker sync handles and mode options, whatwg fs pr #10 for unresolved `move()`, webkit storage policy for quota/eviction, plus local `pagedb` vfs/pager/txn/recovery code. verdict: `fix_first`

ordinary|**findings** 1. `must_fix` — scope / rename + durability exact claim: “persisted, crash-safe manifest maps vfs paths -> physical ids” and “segment promote/tombstone crash window reduces to atomic manifest replacement” in [prd line 60](/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/prd/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-adapter.md:60). why: this moves the hard part from opfs rename into a new metadata database, but the prd does not specify the manifest’s own crash protocol. pagedb’s `vfs` requires `sync_dir` to durably commit metadata changes after renames/creates/removes ([traits.rs line 42](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/vfs/traits.rs:42)), and commit/recovery paths rely on `rename` + `sync_dir` for segment promotion/tombstoning ([write.rs line 766](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/txn/write.rs:766), [reconcile.rs line 84](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/recovery/reconcile.rs:84)). “atomic manifest replacement” is not yet a design. concrete fix: add a manifest mini-protocol: file name(s), a/b slots or wal, seq/checksum/mac, flush ordering, how `sync_dir(path)` maps to manifest flush, recovery selection of the last valid manifest, and gc for orphan physical ids. add oracle cases for torn manifest write, manifest/data mismatch, rename during reconcile, and crash after manifest update before physical gc. 2. `must_fix` — export / portability / debuggability exact claim: opfs eviction means the adapter exposes “the measurement hooks and documented export path consumers need” in [prd line 50](/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/prd/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-adapter.md:50), while v1 acceptance includes native-web receipt determinism in [prd line 142](/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/prd/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-adapter.md:142). why: with id-named physical files, the opfs directory is no longer the logical pagedb tree. `list_dir("seg")` and reconcile must be manifest-backed logical views, not physical directory walks ([reconcile.rs line 166](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/recovery/reconcile.rs:166)); raw “tar the opfs directory” or opfs explorer inspection would be manifest-dependent and not receipt-parity evidence by itself. concrete fix: add an explicit manifest-aware export/import/debug flow and success criterion. state whether raw physical export is supported only with manifest + resolver tooling, or whether v1 must produce a native-compatible logical tree export. 3. `must_fix` — multi-tab reader typed errors exact claim: “pagedb’s readonly/observer open modes over opfs are declared unsupported (typed error)” in [prd line 37](/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/prd/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-adapter.md:37) and [line 74](/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/prd/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-adapter.md:74). why: `dbmode` is handled above the vfs; `vfs::open` does not know whether this is standalone, readonly, or observer. `open_read_only` probes `.writer.lock` then calls `lock_shared(".frozen_readers.lock")`; observer calls `lock_shared(".observers.lock")` ([open.rs line 468](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/txn/db/open.rs:468), [open.rs line 539](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/txn/db/open.rs:539)). without an upstream mode-capability hook, opfs cannot reliably return the promised typed “mode unsupported” error. concrete fix: specify the upstream api change: either add backend capability checks to `open_with_mode`, preserve `unsupported` from sentinel lock acquisition, or add opfs-specific constructors/bootstrap guards. acceptance needs tests for `db::open_read_only(opfsvfs)` and `db::open_observer(opfsvfs)` returning the intended typed error. 4. `should_fix` — typed error taxonomy exact claim: “the current enum has no such variants” in [prd line 63](/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/prd/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-adapter.md:63). why: current `pagedberror` already has `quota`, `nospace`, `alreadylocked`, and `unsupported` ([errors.rs line 15](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/errors.rs:15), [errors.rs line 41](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/errors.rs:41), [errors.rs line 117](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/errors.rs:117)). the real gap is browser-storage mapping and worker-ownership, not a wholly absent storage/lock taxonomy. concrete fix: reword to: “dom quota should map to existing `nospace`/`quota` or a new vfs-storage variant by upstream decision; workerownership likely needs a new or mapped variant.” platform cross-check: the revised web locks fail-fast story matches mdn’s `ifavailable` behavior, and sync access handles are still dedicated-worker opfs handles with exclusive file locking per mdn/whatwg. sources: [mdn filesystemsyncaccesshandle](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-us/docs/web/api/filesystemsyncaccesshandle), [mdn lockmanager.request](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-us/docs/web/api/lockmanager/request), [whatwg file system spec](https://fs.spec.whatwg.org/#api-filesystemfilehandle-createsyncaccesshandle). verdict: fix_first

ordinary|**findings** 1. `must_fix` — scope, manifest crash protocol / durability mapping exact claim: `sync_dir()` maps to manifest flush and commit ordering `renames -> manifest/dir-sync` is preserved. why: normal `writetxn::commit` propagates `sync_dir()` errors, but other source paths still ignore metadata durability failures after header commits or journal actions. examples: [segment.rs](</users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/txn/db/segment.rs:272>) ignores `sync_dir("seg")`; [journal.rs](</users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/recovery/journal.rs:262>) makes `execute_journal_actions` return `()` and ignores rename errors. with manifest-backed rename, those ignored errors become ignored manifest-commit errors. concrete fix: add a `/spec` task to audit and patch all post-header metadata paths (`txn/db/segment.rs`, `compaction/helpers.rs`, `recovery/reconcile.rs`, `txn/db/gc.rs`, `recovery/journal.rs`, `txn/db/snapshot.rs`) so manifest flush failures are either propagated or deliberately kept journal-retryable before clearing completion state. 2. `must_fix` — scope, cross-tab single-writer / reader-mode typed error exact claim: option `(a)` “backend returns `pagedberror::unsupported` from `lock_shared` on the reader sentinel paths (works today, zero trait changes)”. why: it does not work today. `open_with_mode` erases every `lock_shared` error into `alreadylocked` for both readonly and observer at [open.rs](</users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/txn/db/open.rs:552>) and [open.rs](</users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/txn/db/open.rs:559>). readonly also probes `.writer.lock` first and returns `writerpresent` before the frozen-reader sentinel path when a writer is active at [open.rs](</users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/txn/db/open.rs:544>). concrete fix: remove “works today” and make `/spec` choose either a mode-capability hook/bootstrap guard before lock acquisition, or a source change that preserves `unsupported` through `open_with_mode` and tests both writer-present and writer-absent readonly cases. 3. `should_fix` — scope, vectored-write atomicity strategy exact claim: pre-extend + `navigator.storage.estimate()` “structurally” moves mid-write quota failure ahead of data writes. why: `estimate()` is not a reservation and cannot make opfs quota availability stable across the subsequent writes. pre-extension can reduce one failure mode, but it does not satisfy the `vfsfile` all-or-nothing contract by itself. the deferral to upstream is fine; the mitigation claim is too strong and could lead `/spec` to underbuild the staging/swap path. concrete fix: reword this as advisory only: `/spec` must either implement staging/swap for observable multi-write atomicity or obtain an explicit upstream carveout with a browser test that demonstrates the accepted residual behavior. 4. `should_fix` — success criteria, browser engine suites / snapshot exact claim: pagedb engine-level suites including `snapshot` pass against real opfs in headless browsers. why: current snapshot/export/apply code is native-only: `src/snapshot/mod.rs` gates `apply` and `export` behind `cfg(not(target_arch = "wasm32"))` at [mod.rs](</users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/snapshot/mod.rs:3>), and `txn/db/snapshot.rs` is also `cfg(not(target_arch = "wasm32"))`. the new logical-tree export requirement is valid, but it is not the existing snapshot suite as-is. concrete fix: split the criterion: native snapshot suite remains native; opfs gets a new manifest-aware logical export/import oracle or a vfs/bridge-backed browser export path, with export-to-native receipt parity as the acceptance test. verdict: fix_first

ordinary|found must-fix issues. read-only review only; no files changed. 1. `must_fix` — in scope / cross-tab single-writer exact claim: option `(c) a bootstrap-level guard before lock acquisition` is listed as a valid /spec option while the acceptance test requires `db::open_read_only(opfsvfs)` and `db::open_observer(opfsvfs)` to return the intended typed error in writer-present and writer-absent states. why: a js/bootstrap guard cannot make the direct upstream `db::open_*` apis pass. source confirms `open_with_mode` probes `.writer.lock` first for readonly and returns `writerpresent`, then maps reader `lock_shared` failures to `alreadylocked`: [open.rs](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/txn/db/open.rs:539). concrete fix: remove option `(c)`, or rewrite it as an upstream pre-lock capability check inside `db::open_with_mode`/a `vfs` capability hook. the check must run before the writer-present probe if the intended opfs reader error is `unsupported`. 2. `must_fix` — in scope / manifest crash protocol exact claim: “sync_dir-error-swallowing call sites” includes `recovery/journal.rs:262`. why: [recovery/journal.rs](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/recovery/journal.rs:262) has no `sync_dir`; it swallows `mkdir_all` and `rename` failures and returns `()`. [txn/db/snapshot.rs](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/txn/db/snapshot.rs:405) then calls it and proceeds toward clearing `apply_journal_root_page_id`, so a manifest/rename failure can be marked complete instead of retryable. also, `recovery/gc.rs` has a swallowed `sync_dir` not in the audit list. concrete fix: broaden this from “sync_dir-error-swallowing” to “post-header metadata side-effect swallowing”: audit `mkdir_all`, `rename`, `remove`, and `sync_dir` in the listed files, add `recovery/gc.rs`, and require `execute_journal_actions` to return `result` or explicitly preserve retryability before journal completion is cleared. 3. `should_fix` — in scope / browser test + bench harness exact claim: browser matrix still includes engine suite `snapshot` and durability oracle `journal replay`. why: success criterion 1 correctly excludes snapshot from the browser matrix, and source confirms snapshot export/apply plus `txn/db/snapshot.rs` are native-only with `#[cfg(not(target_arch = "wasm32"))]`: [snapshot.rs](/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb/src/txn/db/snapshot.rs:4). the prd also says normal commits use `apply_journal_root_page_id: 0` and rely on reconcile, not journal replay. concrete fix: update the harness bullet to match criteria 1-2: remove `snapshot` from the browser engine-suite list and replace `journal replay` with the browser logical export/import plus reconcile/manifest oracles. if native snapshot tests remain required, name them as native-only upstream regression coverage. verdict: fix_first

ordinary|no must_fix findings. i re-read the current prd and cross-checked the load-bearing claims against `/users/andrew/src/lodefolio/data/databases/pagedb`. the pass-4 fixes converged: reader-mode `unsupported` really is masked above the vfs today, metadata side-effect swallowing is now called out where it matters, snapshot is correctly excluded from the browser matrix, and the harness now targets reconcile/manifest/export-import oracles instead of pretending normal commits exercise journal replay. one note i would not hold: the prd’s “all post-header metadata side effects” wording is slightly broader than its file list, because `txn/write.rs` also has post-header mkdir/rename/sync_dir operations. but those currently propagate errors, and the prd already requires vfs-level manifest semantics plus oracle coverage, so this does not materially misdirect `/spec`. verdict: ship

ordinary|{"verdict":"reject","summary":"the plan still has a false crash-oracle expectation: two required worker-kill beacons can legitimately recover a newer valid a/b slot even though the commit path had not returned. it also leaves contradictory acceptance language around the now-intentional vectored-write contract carveout.","findings":[{"severity":"high","title":"pre-sync kill oracle expects an impossible prior-state guarantee","body":"task 7 requires real worker termination at phase beacons and says every case must reopen to exactly the last committed state with no uncommitted state visible, including after header write before header sync and mid-manifest-commit. the referenced header code writes a fully encoded higher-seq header before calling `sync()` (`src/pager/header.rs:127-143`), and the plan's manifest protocol likewise publishes by writing the inactive higher-seq slot and then loading the highest valid seq (task 3, lines 165-166). because neither protocol records a separate durable 'flushed' marker, worker termination after a complete write but before flush can leave a valid newer slot that recovery will select. the oracle will either fail nondeterministically or force a stronger guarantee than the selected a/b design can provide.","file":"/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/plans/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-sync-adapter.md","line_start":263,"line_end":267,"confidence":0.86,"recommendation":"redefine pre-sync header/manifest beacons as commit-ambiguous old-or-new outcomes with invariants of no torn/mixed state, or add an explicit durable commit marker if strict prior-state recovery is required; split manifest cases into before-write, torn-write, and after-flush expectations."},{"severity":"medium","title":"acceptance text still denies the chosen carveout","body":"goal verification still requires the complete conformance suite to pass with zero skipped or carved-out cases, and task 9 initially says contract carveouts are 'none expected'. that conflicts with task 4's explicit prd option (b) vectored-write carveout and task 9's later requirement to document that carveout in the pr description. the result is a definition-of-done that can be read as full `vfsfile::write_at_vectored` all-or-nothing compliance even though the plan intentionally narrows the contract from `src/vfs/traits.rs:64-66`.","file":"/users/andrew/code/pres/brow/pagedb-opfs/docs/plans/2026-07-02-pagedb-opfs-sync-adapter.md","line_start":80,"line_end":82,"confidence":0.92,"recommendation":"update goal verification and task 9 objective text to name the single accepted vectored-write carveout, and add an explicit check that trait docs plus the pr draft describe the narrowed contract and the mid-vectored-write oracle evidence."}],"next_steps":["revise task 7 oracle expectations before implementation so the crash tests encode the actual a/b publication semantics.","normalize all acceptance and pr-packaging text so the vectored-write carveout is explicit rather than contradicted."]}
